content:
# Weekly Review: Jørgen | June 14–20, 2026

---

## 1. Shift Impact

The mixed shift pattern created predictable but manageable disruption this week. Your two day shifts (06-17, 06-18) required early starts, and the data shows it: the night before your second day shift you logged only 5.3 hours of sleep (score 58), your lowest of the week. Body battery dropped to 85 on 06-18 — still functional, but noticeably lower than the 100s you hit on both evening-shift days (06-16, 06-17). Evening shifts clearly suit your chronobiology better right now. HRV peaked at 76 on 06-16 (evening shift) and held solid at 73 on 06-17, suggesting your body was genuinely recovered during that stretch. The red flag is 06-18: 5.3h sleep, HRV back down to 66, RHR nudging up to 44 bpm — a clear day-shift hangover. The recovery nap on 06-19 (12.3 hours, HRV 69, BB 100) confirms you were carrying a sleep debt into your day off. Eating was also chaotic around day shifts — 06-18 produced your highest calorie day by far (3,102 kcal), likely compensatory eating after poor sleep and a demanding shift.

---

## 2. Training

Two runs totalling 11.3 km is a reasonable volume for a week with five working days, but the timing was good, not great. Your 6.4 km run on 06-15 came on a high-HRV (69), high-BB (92) day before an evening shift — that's the right call. The 4.9 km run on 06-17 was solid given strong recovery metrics (HRV 73, BB 100). No sessions were done on the two most fatigued days (06-18, 06-20), which shows decent self-awareness. The cycling on 06-20 was light commute-style activity (2 × 6.4 km), not training load. Zero interval sessions this week is worth noting — if progressive fitness is the goal, you need at least one quality session per week, not just easy aerobic volume.

---

## 3. Nutrition

Calorie average of 1,792 kcal sits 192 kcal above your 1,600 target — not disastrous, but the variance is the real problem. You swung from 1,224 kcal (06-17) to 3,102 kcal (06-18) on back-to-back days. That 06-18 spike almost certainly reflects poor-sleep hunger hormones and shift-fatigue eating, not intentional fuelling. Protein averaged 118.5 g against a 150 g target, falling short every single day except 06-17 (164.9 g) — which ironically was your lowest-calorie day, meaning you can hit the protein number when you're structured. Two days had no logged nutrition data at all (06-14) or incomplete logging, which makes it impossible to coach accurately. Prioritise consistency in tracking above all else.

---

## 4. Recovery

The overall recovery picture is actually better than it might look on the surface. Average HRV of 69.1 and RHR of 43.6 bpm are healthy baselines for a 28-year-old endurance-capable athlete. Body battery averaged 92.1 at peak, which is strong. The concern is the two dips that cluster around day shifts — both times HRV dropped to 66 and sleep score hit 58. That's a pattern, not a coincidence. Watch the RHR creep on 06-20 (45 bpm, your weekly high) after another short night of 5.7 hours — if that trend continues into next week it signals accumulated fatigue, not just one bad night.

---

## 5. Key Takeaway

**Prepare for your day shifts the day before, not the morning of.** Your data shows clearly that 07:00 starts are eroding your sleep and spiking your calories. Next week, when you see a day shift on the schedule, treat the preceding evening as a recovery priority: no late meals, no screens past 21:00, and aim to be in bed by 21:30.
